era_context: Continuation of the Hellenistic period with the Seleucid Empire fractured between Seleucus II and Antiochus Hierax; Ptolemaic Egypt consolidating after Raphia; Rome in the early stages of the Second Punic War preparations; China under Qin Shi Huang's continued consolidation; the Parthian Empire emerging in northeastern Iran.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Parthian Empire (limited contemporary sources; mostly known through later Greek and Roman accounts)

events.title: Parthian Kingdom emergence and consolidation
events.description: The Parthian Kingdom, founded by Arsaces I around 247 BCE, continued its consolidation of power in northeastern Iran during this period. By 226 BCE, Parthia had established itself as a significant regional power, controlling trade routes and resisting Seleucid overlordship. The kingdom would eventually become one of the major powers of the ancient world, rivaling Rome and the Seleucids. However, contemporary sources for this specific year are extremely limited.

sources.name: Strabo, Geography (written ~20 BCE, describing earlier periods)

events.certainty_note: The founding of Parthia is traditionally dated to 247 BCE, but specific events in 226 BCE are not well-documented. This entry reflects the broader era context rather than a specific datable event.
cross_references: Part of the long-term rise of Parthia as a major power (247 BCE – 224 CE)

events.title: Qin military campaigns in southern China
events.description: The Qin Dynasty continued its military expansion into southern territories, including campaigns against the Baiyue (Hundred Yue) peoples and other non-Han populations. These campaigns were part of the broader Qin strategy to extend unified control over all territories within reach. The campaigns were often brutal and met with resistance, but gradually extended Qin administrative control southward.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Qin military sites in southern China

events.certainty_note: The Qin campaigns in the south are well-documented in broad outline, but precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain. This entry represents the general pattern of Qin expansion during the early unified period.
cross_references: Part of Qin territorial consolidation (221–206 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events for 226 BCE have been identified with high confidence in the available sources. The Parthian Kingdom's emergence is traditionally dated to 247 BCE, but the specific trajectory and events of 226 BCE are not well-documented. The Qin campaigns in the south are attested in general terms but not precisely dated to individual years.
historiographic_note: 226 BCE falls in a period of relatively sparse documentation for most of the world. The Hellenistic sources focus on the Mediterranean and Near East; Chinese sources are available but retrospective; Indian, Persian, and other regional sources are minimal or absent. The year appears to be one of consolidation and continuation rather than major documented events, which may reflect both historical reality and the limitations of surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Parthian consolidation eventually led to major conflicts with Rome and the Seleucids
